programs.name: Oregon Health Plan (OHP)
programs.what_it_does: The Oregon Health Plan is Oregon's Medicaid program providing comprehensive healthcare coverage including medical care, dental services, mental health treatment, prescriptions, vision care, and emergency services for low-income seniors. It also covers long-term care services not typically covered by Medicare or traditional health insurance.[2][3]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors aged 65+, adults aged 18-64 with physical disabilities requiring nursing facility level of care, with income at or below 300% of SSI Federal Benefit Rate and assets under $2,000 (single)/$3,000 (couple).[1][3]
programs.benefit_value: Free or low-cost health coverage; covers long-term services and supports; income limits ~$841 single/$1,261 couple (2022, subject to change).[3]

programs.name: APD Medicaid Long Term Services and Supports
programs.what_it_does: Administered by Aging and People with Disabilities (APD), this program provides Medicaid-funded long-term care and medical services including home and community-based services waivers. It supports seniors and disabled individuals to receive care in community settings rather than institutions.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Individuals aged 65+ or 18-64 with physical disabilities requiring nursing facility level of care, meeting Medicaid income and asset limits.[1][3]
programs.benefit_value: Coverage for long-term services like home care, adult foster care, and waiver programs; value depends on assessed needs.

programs.name: Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)
programs.what_it_does: PACE provides comprehensive medical and social services to eligible seniors in community-based settings, including adult day health, transportation, and home care to avoid nursing home placement. In Oregon, it is administered through approved PACE organizations partnering with APD/OHP.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Seniors 55+ certified as needing nursing home level care but able to live safely in the community, enrolled in Medicare and Medicaid.[1]
programs.benefit_value: Full capitated coverage of Medicare and Medicaid services; no additional premiums for dual eligibles.

programs.name: Medicare Savings Programs (MSP) via OHP
programs.what_it_does: Oregon's Medicare Savings Programs, including Qualified Medicare Beneficiary (QMB), Specified Low-Income Medicare Beneficiary (SLMB), and Qualifying Individual (QI), pay Medicare premiums, deductibles, and copays for eligible low-income seniors. Administered through OHP application process.[3]
programs.who_its_for: Medicare beneficiaries with low income and limited assets; specific income thresholds vary (e.g., 100% FPL for QMB, up to 135% for SLMB).[3]
programs.benefit_value: QMB: Full Part A/B cost-sharing; SLMB: Part B premium (~$174.70/mo); QI: Part B premium assistance.

programs.name: SNAP Food Benefits
programs.what_it_does: SNAP provides monthly benefits via EBT card to purchase groceries, helping low-income seniors afford nutritious food. In Oregon, administered by Department of Human Services with deductions for seniors' expenses.[2][7]
programs.who_its_for: Seniors 60+ with income below ~130-200% FPL (net/gross), assets under $4,250 (household).[7]
programs.benefit_value: Average $200-300/month per person; max benefit ~$291/single (2024 rates, adjusted annually).

programs.name: Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP) / Oregon Energy Assistance Program (OEAP)
programs.what_it_does: LIHEAP provides grants for heating bills, repairs, and weatherization; OEAP prevents utility disconnections for low-income households including seniors. Both administered statewide through partner agencies.[2]
programs.who_its_for: Households with income ≤60% state median income, priority for seniors 60+, disabled, families with children.[2]
programs.benefit_value: One-time grants $200-$1,000+ for heating/energy bills; crisis assistance up to $800.

programs.name: Weatherization Assistance Program (WAP)
programs.what_it_does: WAP funds energy efficiency upgrades like insulation, sealing, and furnace repairs to reduce utility costs for low-income homes. Administered through Oregon's Community Action Partnerships.[2]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income households ≤200% FPL, priority for seniors, disabled, and children.[2]
programs.benefit_value: Free home improvements valued at $5,000-$10,000+ per home.

programs.name: Senior Health Insurance Benefits Assistance (SHIBA)
programs.what_it_does: SHIBA offers free, unbiased counseling on Medicare options, enrollment, and appeals through trained volunteers at Area Agencies on Aging. It helps seniors navigate Medicare Savings Programs and supplemental coverage.[5]
programs.who_its_for: All Oregon Medicare beneficiaries 65+, no income requirements.[5]
programs.benefit_value: Free one-on-one counseling; potential savings of thousands via proper Medicare plan selection.

programs.name: Meals on Wheels (via Area Agencies on Aging)
programs.what_it_does: Delivers nutritious meals to homebound seniors, often with friendly visits to reduce isolation. Funded through Older Americans Act and administered by Oregon's 16 AAAs.[2][6]
programs.who_its_for: Homebound seniors 60+ unable to prepare meals, often with low income or health needs.[6]
programs.benefit_value: 5-14 meals/week at $3-$8/suggested donation; free for very low-income.

programs.name: National Family Caregiver Support Program (NFCSP)
programs.what_it_does: Provides support services like respite care, training, and counseling to family caregivers of seniors. Administered by Oregon AAAs under Older Americans Act.[6]
programs.who_its_for: Family caregivers of adults 60+ or grandparents raising children.[6]
programs.benefit_value: Respite grants up to $1,000/year; training and support groups at low/no cost.

programs.name: Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP)
programs.what_it_does: Offers part-time community service jobs with training to unemployed low-income seniors leading to unsubsidized employment. Operated by AAAs and partners.[6]
programs.who_its_for: Unemployed 55+ with income ≤125% FPL, Oregon residents.[6]
programs.benefit_value: Minimum wage work ~20 hrs/week; paid training and placement services.

programs.name: Legal Aid Services for Seniors (via Oregon Law Center / AAAs)
programs.what_it_does: Provides free legal help for issues like benefits, housing, abuse, and consumer problems. Available through Oregon Law Center, Disability Rights Oregon, and AAA legal clinics.[2]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors 60+, priority for public benefits and elder rights cases.[2]
programs.benefit_value: Free legal representation; resolves issues worth thousands in benefits/housing.

programs.name: Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program
programs.what_it_does: Advocates for residents in long-term care facilities, investigating complaints and ensuring quality care. Administered statewide by AAAs under Older Americans Act.[6]
programs.who_its_for: Residents of nursing homes, assisted living, adult foster homes in Oregon.[6]
programs.benefit_value: Free advocacy resolving ~80% complaints; protects rights and improves care.

programs.name: Senior Guardianship Assistance Program (Senior GAP)
programs.what_it_does: Senior GAP offers guardianship, conservatorship, and money management services for self-sufficient seniors struggling with financial affairs. It supports safe independent living through targeted assistance.[2]
programs.who_its_for: Seniors aged 60+ who are otherwise self-sufficient but need help managing finances or legal protections.
programs.whats_unique: Oregon-specific program by Impact NW providing holistic guardianship and financial aid tailored to vulnerable yet independent seniors, not a standard federal offering.
programs.benefit_value: Free or low-cost guardianship and money management; supports 1,500 individuals annually with no fixed dollar amount.

programs.name: Oregon Senior Tuition Waiver (OSU/UO Audit Program)
programs.what_it_does: Allows seniors to audit university classes tuition-free on a space-available basis. Provides access to higher education for personal enrichment without cost.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Oregon seniors aged 65 and older interested in auditing classes at Oregon State University or University of Oregon.
programs.whats_unique: State-funded fee waivers unique to Oregon public universities, enabling free class auditing for seniors—varies by state with Oregon's specific implementation.
programs.benefit_value: Full tuition waiver for auditing classes; value depends on course fees avoided (typically hundreds per class).

programs.name: Health Related Social Needs Benefits (HSRN)
programs.what_it_does: Provides extra assistance with housing, food, or climate needs like air conditioners for health-related issues. Targets those with complex health needs, housing instability, or life transitions.[4]
programs.who_its_for: Oregon Health Plan or Health Share of Oregon members with complicated health needs, homelessness risk, or major life changes.
programs.whats_unique: Oregon state program integrated with OHP offering targeted non-medical supports beyond standard Medicaid, focused on social determinants of health.
programs.benefit_value: Varies by need (e.g., housing aid, appliances); no fixed amount, case-by-case support.
